title: Building a two-bay compost bin from pallets or timber
subtitle: Why two bays outperform one, and how to size and build them
excerpt: A single bin fills up and cannot be turned. Two bays (one active, one maturing) let you fill one side, then fork the contents into the second bay to finish while the first fills again. Each bay needs roughly 1 cubic metre of volume to generate enough heat for active decomposition.
sourceNotes: RHS composting guidance on bin sizing and turning. Charles Dowding no-dig method on compost system design. WRAP (Waste & Resources Action Programme) household composting guidance.

content.text: The minimum useful compost bin is 1 m x 1 m x 1 m (1 cubic metre). Below this volume, the heap loses heat too quickly for active hot composting. A two-bay system doubles the volume and enables a simple rotation: fill bay A while bay B matures, then fork A into B (aerating and mixing in the process) when A is full, and start filling A again.

Four standard EUR pallets (1,200 x 800 mm) make two bays sharing a central divider. Stand three pallets on edge to form a U-shape (two sides and a back). Wire or bolt adjacent pallets together at the corners. Add a fourth pallet as the central divider, wired to the back pallet. The front of each bay is left open for access, or can use loose boards slid into slots to retain material. Pallets marked HT (heat-treated) are safe for composting use; avoid any marked MB (methyl bromide).

A more durable option: four corner posts (75 x 75 mm pressure-treated timber, 1.2 m long, driven 300 mm into the ground) and horizontal boards nailed or screwed to the outside. Leave 10-20 mm gaps between boards for aeration. The central partition is two posts with boards either side. Use boards 150-200 mm wide for the front of each bay: removable slats make loading and unloading easier.

alternately, roughly 1:1 by volume. A heap that smells of ammonia has too many greens: add browns. A heap that is dry and not decomposing has too many browns: add greens and water. The heap should feel as moist as a wrung-out sponge. Cover the top with cardboard or a lid to retain moisture in dry weather and prevent waterlogging in wet weather.

Turn bay A into bay B when bay A is full and has cooled: typically 4-8 weeks after filling. Forking material from one bay into the other aerates the heap and moves the cooler outer material into the hotter centre. After turning, bay A can start filling again. Bay B material is ready to use as finished compost after a further 4-8 weeks of maturation, when it is dark, crumbly, and smells earthy with no recognisable inputs.
